data.title: Blue-Chip Dividends Entering Bargain Territory: 2 Names To Buy
data.description:
Since the new President took office, the economy has become more uncertain. On the one hand, there is a justified assumption of experiencing a decent boost to the economy through an easier political decision-making and the promise to stimulate local supply chains. But on the other hand, we see such things as the introduction of tariffs and increasing geopolitical tensions that have completely opposite effects on the economy.

This kind of uncertainty has muted the growth expectations that the market had priced in across various indices. The Nasdaq-100 (QQQ) is the best example to take in order to see these effects. Namely, the index is down ~8% on a YTD basis and the underlying volatility or the VIX has become rather elevated.

In fact, it is not only pure-play growth companies that have experienced a decline in their valuations, but also some value-oriented names as well. The S&P 500 (SPY), which has a decent concentration of value stocks, is down ~4% on a YTD basis.

In other words, the recent market dynamics have created some interesting opportunities for long-term investors to consider.

In my view, we currently also have the right environment for high quality and income-based investing. Since the overall growth prospects seem quite challenging and the odds for a recession have increased, the equity story for defensive value plays has improved accordingly.

If we can get both things - high quality factor and relatively low/attractive valuations - then I think we should at least dedicate some time to consider such opportunities.

In the article below, I will elaborate on two picks, which embody both of these factors, including an enticing yield angle, which is critical for me as a prudent and long-term income investor.

#1 - Alexandria Real Estate Equities

Alexandria Real Estate Equities, Inc. (ARE) is a high-quality equity REIT that has been completely misunderstood by the market.

Theoretically, we could find grounds for having ARE discounted:

  • Unfavorable supply/demand balance in the office space (driven by the work-from-home dynamic).
  • Higher-for-longer imposing damaging refinancing risk.
  • AI decreasing the need for a lab space.

As a result of such a consensus in the market, ARE is trading at a dirt cheap multiple. Here is a visual representation of the P/CF dynamic.

Over the past three years, ARE's P/CF multiple has declined by almost 70%. We have to also keep in mind that the actual valuation level has decreased even more than this because during the 3-year period ARE has managed to register a growing FFO per share figure.

The question is whether all of this is justified.

In my humble opinion, the answer is a clear no.

First, none of the three aforementioned (negative) aspects have a meaningful impact on ARE and, second, ARE's fundamentals remain just as strong as in the period before the share price started to plunge.

Here are the key facts that prove that.

First, ARE has an upper investment grade credit rating, which is a very rare thing in the equity REIT space. It allows ARE to source in cheap debt and have the necessary flexibility on the maturity structure end. For example, currently, ARE has only 14% of its total debt expiring over the next three-year period. Hence, the negative refinancing risk here is very immaterial.

Second, ARE's leasing performance clearly indicates that there is a strong and healthy demand for its properties. Even though the leasing volume in 2024 was 17% above that of the previous year, the REIT still managed to secure leases at attractive spreads, while maintaining an excellent occupancy (i.e., 94.6%). For example, the releasing cash basis spread of 7.2% with a ~9.5 years of average lease maturity term does not give the impression that ARE's properties have gone out of vogue.

Third, and this is somewhat related to the previous point, ARE is a strongly growing business; and the financials prove that. In 2024, the business generated FFO per share growth of 5.6% and the Q4 FFO per share figure advanced by 6% on a year-to-year basis.

While the market has priced in a slight FFO decline in 2025 (due to temporary vacancies, non-core asset divestitures, and organic CapEx projects that start to come online in 2026), the future trajectory of the FFO performance looks solid.

In a nutshell, the P/FFO of 9.8x seems way too low relative to what we get by buying ARE.

The business is growing, backed by a robust capital structure and offers an enticing yield of 5.7%, which is underpinned by one of the most conservative FFO payout ratios in the sector (i.e., ~55%).

#2 - Brookfield Infrastructure Partners

Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP)(BIP.UN:CA)(BIPC) has a similar story to ARE. It is a robust business with steadily growing cash flows that has been subject to unfavorable repricing by the market. Over the past three years, BIP's share (or unit) price has dropped by ~33%. During this exact time period, the FFO per share has consistently increased without suffering any hiccups in the process.

Currently, BIP's P/FFO stands at 9.1x and the distribution yield is 5.6%.

The theoretical reason for the decline in BIP's valuation is tied to the combination of higher-for-longer interest rate scenario and inflation uncertainties. The higher the interest rates, the more challenging it is for BIP to achieve positive final-investment-decisions (i.e., it makes new infrastructure projects more expensive, lowering the demand from the buyers' side). Plus, there is the same argument of unfavorable refinancings just as in ARE's case. Since BIP is inherently a heavy-leverage business, these dynamics have to be taken seriously.

However, if we look deeper into BIP, we will arrive at the same conclusion as above for ARE: i.e., the market has got this wrong.

First, BIP has a strong investment grade credit rating and 90% of the borrowings are fixed, which in combination with ~8-year weighted average maturity is a significant mitigant for the refinancing risk. Furthermore, the lion's share of BIP's debt is assumed on a non-recourse basis, which means that in case any specific project goes belly up, BIP can just as easily hand the keys back to the lenders without having to cover the losses through its own balance sheet. It has not used it before in any material fashion, but still, it is worth factoring in this nuance.

Second, BIP's FFO payout is 67%, which helps drive sustainable growth. In other words, through notable FFO retention, BIP can assume incremental CapEx spend in a more sustainable (not as debt dependent) fashion. Here, we have to also appreciate the fact that BIP can access additional capital from completed project sales. For instance, last year, the business accessed $2 billion by selling high IRR project stakes. This year, management plans to utilize this source more aggressively (which makes sense given that BIP had many completed projects in 2024), thereby further relaxing the pressure on the balance sheet.

Third, and this is important, I think it would be logical to assume a higher FFO growth rate going forward than in the past. Since 2020, the average FFO per share growth has been ~8%, which is already attractive enough in the context of a single digit P/FFO multiple.

So, the reason why I think that BIP could in the medium-term hit a low double-digit FFO per unit growth mark is nicely captured in the chart below:

As we can see, there is a significant chunk of CapEx that is earmarked towards much higher growth areas than the typical/traditional infrastructure, such as utilities and transport. The data front is what can generate high double-digit growth, thus pushing the overall portfolio results higher.

To illustrate this point, we can take a look at the data segment's performance in 2024. In 2024, BIP registered an FFO growth of 21% in the data segment. All of these cash flows stem from corporate offtakers (parties) that have long-term and CPI-linked contracts with BIP.

The bottom line

Oftentimes, when the market becomes a more volatile place, there are some attractive opportunities that emerge. In fact, these negative moves in the market can make already unfairly priced securities even more discounted.

This is exactly what has happened both for ARE and BIP.

As elaborated above in the article, in my view, both businesses are misunderstood by the market and being priced as low-quality securities despite having robust capital structures and defensive (and growing) cash flows.

data.title: Waste Management: Fast Grower, But Beware Of High Debt And Overvaluation
data.description:
Waste Management (NYSE:WM) is a simple company with a simple business model, that has seen great growth over the past few years. Their stock price has skyrocketed by 164.38% just over the past year, and so far, it doesn't show a sign of stopping. Sadly, based on my analysis, I do have to rate the company as a HOLD rather than a BUY, mainly due to their current overvaluation and high debt. In this short article, we will take a look at their latest Q4'24 earnings report and analyze their fundamentals through our 9 Pillar Analysis to finally calculate their intrinsic value.

Analysis

Q4'24 Earnings

First things first, let's start by looking at WM's short-term achievements. In Q4, the company actually reported a miss on their Non-GAAP EPS, reporting it at $1.70, $0.10 lower than the estimates of $1.80.

Their revenue, although increasing by 12.80% YOY, was also reported as a small miss by $20.00M, expected at $5.89B but reported at $5.87B. Regarding the net income of the company, that has increased by 21.29% YOY, from $493.00M to $598.00M.

The company is also innovating, reporting that the money saved from a 1000 expected job cuts will go towards renewable energy plants, as well as be returned to investors through FCF, and spent on artificial intelligence through the next years.

All of this news is great; financial growth shows no sign of stopping, and with innovation, it could be expected that revenue will start increasing at an even higher rate. But for now, these are just speculations that aren't based on evidence.

9 Pillars

Therefore, as I am a long-term value investor, rather than basing my investments on short-term news and trends, I like to base them on long-term historical fundamentals. By going through the 9 pillars, we will find all the flaws WM has, as well as its strengths, so let's get to it.

Above, you can see that the company gets a rating of 6/9, with three pillars in the red. The 5Y PE and P/FCF, along with the debt levels. First, let's take a look at the company's valuation.

Valuation (Pillars 1 and 2)

The 5Y PE is sitting rather high, at 27.04 which in my eyes is expensive for a waste management company, though it is still bearable. The issue though is that currently, the PE is sitting even higher, at 34.33, increasing at a 5Y CAGR of 6.06%. This means that through the past years, investors have been paying more and more for the company, inflating its stock price. Below, you can see a graph depicting the 5Y PE growth.

Regarding the P/FCF, that is currently sitting at a whopping 52.19, which in my opinion screams overvaluation. Over the past 5Y, this ratio has been growing at a YOY rate of 21.28%, which translates to investors willing to pay 21.28% more for the company's yearly net income every single year.

Financial Growth

Now, of course, this growth in premium is to an extent justified because the revenue of the company has been growing at a 5Y CAGR of 7.88%, increasing from $15.22B in 2020, to $22.06B in 2024, and accounting for a total growth of 44.94%

The main deal though is the net income, which has grown by 83.56% over the past 5 years, growing at a CAGR of 13.53% from $1.50B in 2020, to $2.75B in 2024. In my eyes, this is some phenomenal growth; I just don't agree with the current cost of the financials.

Even though the net income and revenue have been growing really well, the FCF has been somewhat lagging behind, growing by only 21.90% over the past 5Y, from $1.77B in 2020, to $2.16B in 2024.

Overall, WM is showing off some great growth, but as I already said, I don't think it deserves such a high valuation.

Debt (Pillar 9)

A further issue I have with the company is their really high debt levels, especially with their slow-growing FCF. Currently, their LTL / 5Y FCF - Dividends ratio is sitting at 7.77, which is much higher than the "lower than 3" ratio I would like to see. Essentially, what this means is that with the company's 5Y average yearly FCF ($2.00B), it would take them about 36 years to pay off all their LTL ($30.06B) while paying out their dividends.

What I like even less is that WM hasn't been at all paying their LTL off; instead, they have been growing them at a CAGR of 11.27%, from $18.34B in 2020, to the current number of $30.06B. In my eyes, this puts the company in a vulnerable position if there were to come financial hardship. Moreover, with such high debt and P/FCF ratio, their value decreases drastically, which we will see in the DCF model, which we will go through at the end of the analysis.

Dividends (Pillar 8)

Talking about debt and FCF, let's take a look at the company's dividend sustainability. Their 5Y FCF payout ratio is sitting at a decent level of 52.48%, which means that after paying out their dividends, the company still has enough FCF to push into innovation and the previously mentioned paying down of debt.

With their dividends seeming quite safe, WM is looking like a great company for dividend growth investors, mainly thanks to their 5Y dividend CAGR of 8.35%.

Remaining Pillars

Before calculating the intrinsic value of the company, let's first look at their profitability and share buybacks. Something I like is that their profit margin has been increasing at a CAGR of 4.97%, from 9.83% in 2020 to 12.45% in 2024. Their ROIC has also been nice and stable, keeping at a 5Y average of 9.57%.

Regarding their share buybacks, they haven't been doing much of them in 2024, because, as we know, they have been trading quite overvalued. What I like though is that through 2022 to 2023, they have bought back around 3.82% of their shares outstanding, returning great value to investors.

Overall, I would say that the company is looking healthy, except for their high debt. Their overvaluation is a whole other story, though. It doesn't mean that when a business is good and growing, you can overpay for it. I would personally rather buy a cheaper company, and wait for a good, overvalued business to drop in price before potentially buying into it.

Intrinsic Value

Anyway, now that we have gone through the fundamentals, let's figure out the intrinsic value of the company, which, based on the PE and P/FCF, I believe will come out lower than the current price. We will kick off the valuation, as always, with the Graham's Formula.

As you can see above, to get the intrinsic value, I first plugged in the 5Y EPS of $5.39. After that, I predicted a 5Y EPS CAGR of 15.67%, which in my eyes is the most realistic EPS growth the company could experience in the future. After combining these two values and plugging them into the formula alongside the other values you see on the diagram, I got an intrinsic value of $170.16, 27.02% lower than the current trading price of $233.15.

The second valuation model we will look to is the DCF model, where I will plug in a 10Y + FCF CAGR at 6.30%, and combine it with a discount rate of 7.00% (my expected YOY return). After adding up all the discounted FCF, we have to subtract the debt, and that is where we run into a problem.

As I mentioned above, the excessive debt of the company will cause problems in its valuation. It currently makes up more than 33.33% of the company's discounted FFCF. Therefore, when we subtract it, accounting for the low cash and cash equivalents, the intrinsic value comes out to only $93.89. If there was no debt, the value would be $155.91.

For the third valuation model, we will compare WM to two of its competitors in the Multiples Valuation: Republic Services and Rollins. We will take these companies' PE ratios and average them out to a whopping 47.18. This shows that compared to its industry, WM might after all not be as overvalued as we thought, as when we multiply this PE with the current EPS of WM sitting at $6.81, we will get an intrinsic value of $321.30, quite higher than the current price

Finally, we will look to the alternative DDM, where I will predict the future dividend CAGR at 8.00% and get an intrinsic value of $256.49.

After averaging out the four intrinsic values, we will arrive to the ultimate intrinsic value sitting at $210.52, 9.83% lower than the current price of $233.47. With a margin of safety of 10.00% our acceptable buy price would be $189.47, which is the price at which I have the company on my watch list.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I have to rate Waste Management as a HOLD. This decision is based on their current overvaluation and high debt, though it is softened by the company's high financial growth and overall healthy financials with great dividend value. I believe that the company has a bright future ahead; I just trust that based on its current overvaluation, it might soon face a crush, which, if that were to happen, I would certainly return and re-rate the company, possibly giving them a BUY rating.

data.title: PVH Q4: Financial Improvements Aren't Big Enough (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
In complete contrast with my Buy rating on it, the owner of brands like Tommy Hilfiger and Calvin Klein, PVH Corp. (PVH), has dropped by 27% since the last time I covered the stock in August last year and has fallen slightly more in the past 12 months (see chart below).

But following its full year 2024 (year ending February 2025) results at the end of March, it jumped by nothing less than 18% in a single day. Could this finally be the start of a secular of rise in PVH? Let's find out.

Non-GAAP EPS makes notable gains...

The highlight of PVH's latest numbers is the earnings per share [EPS], specifically the non-GAAP EPS. This number was supported by gains due to restructuring and income tax gains among others, which reflect as follows:

  • At USD $11.74, it slightly exceeded expectations, with the guidance range of USD $11.55-11.70. Compared to the midpoint of the range, it was around 1% higher.
  • The final number is even more impressive when seen in the context of the fact that the guidance itself was upgraded through the year. The midpoint of the eventual guidance range was 6.9% higher than the initial range of USD 10.75-11. It follows then, that the actual number has taken a notable leap from there, ahead of the initial guidance by ~8%.
  • The non-GAAP EPS also grew by ~10% year-on-year (YoY) despite a contraction in revenues.
  • In Q4 2024 too, the non-GAAP EPS delivered a surprise. It came in 4.6% ahead of the midpoint of the range, though in this case it showed a 12% YoY contraction.

...but GAAP EPS is a mixed bag

The GAAP EPS doesn't paint as positive a picture, though it isn't bad either. At USD 10.56, the figure just about made it to the guidance range of USD 10.55-10.7. Similarly, the number for Q4 2024 was at USD 2.83, compared to the guidance range of USD 2.83-2.98.

Both the full year and Q4 2024 GAAP figures, however, were smaller than those seen last year. This is particularly so for the Q4 2024 number, which dropped by 38% YoY, even while the 2024 figure was down by a more acceptable 1.9%.

Operating margin and revenues as expected

With respect to the remainder of the results, the outcomes were as broadly as expected for both the operating margins and revenues. The details are as follows:

  • Non-GAAP operating margin on point.

The non-GAAP operating margin was almost on point, at 10%, compared to the guidance of 10.1%, but the GAAP margin was a bit lower at 8.9%, compared with the guidance of 9.2%. Even though profits could have benefited from the reduction in operating expenses, the decline in revenues turned out to be the overwhelming factor here.

  • Revenue declined as expected.

PVH Corp saw a 6% decline in revenue on a constant currency basis. This is at the lower end of the guidance range contraction of 6-7% or to put it another way, the decline was a shade lower than the midpoint of the range, which is some consolation. The revenue decline in the final quarter (Q4 2024), at 5% year-on-year (YoY), was actually smaller than the projected guidance range of 6-7%.

Despite the numbers being on target, though, it's hard to get past the fact that the company's sales are shrinking. This is troubling since only part of the decline is explained by the sale of the Heritage Brands' intimates segment. The company's big brands, Tommy Hilfiger and Calvin Klein, also saw a drop in revenues by 4.9% and 1.5% respectively.

Outlook and market multiples

The company's outlook for 2025 shows improvement, though. For one, it expects the revenue contraction to stop now, projecting them "to be flat to increase slightly." The non-GAAP operating margin is forecast to remain unchanged from 2024, while the non-GAAP EPS is expected to land in the range of USD 12.4-12.75. At the midpoint of the range, this reflects a 7.1% increase from 2024.

Assuming that PVH's EPS in fact comes in at the midpoint of the guidance range, the forward non-GAAP price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio comes in at just 6.2x. Not only is this lower than the 9.5x level it was at the last I checked, thanks to the price decline since, but it's also lower than the stock's five-year average of 10.6x. To put this in perspective, if the stock were to go back to trading at the five-year average, the price would have to rise by over 71%.

Can PVH make gains now?

The question now is whether PVH is in a position to make the kind of gains suggested by the forward P/E. Considering the risks, maybe not. But that doesn't mean it can't make gains at all.

A key risk right now is related to the consumer discretionary sector. Of all the major sectors, it's the only sector other than technology to see a weakening in performance year-to-date [YTD] (see chart below). This isn't for no reason either. Mild cooling off in the US economy was already expected for 2025 even last year, however, trade tariffs can have an additional impact on it. Specifically, they can put upward pressure on inflation, which has shown some resistance to softening in recent months in any case.

Next, the company hasn't been able to meet the big targets as per its restructuring plan called PVH+. Its revenues, for example, in 2025 are at best expected to grow just a tad, which would be around 30% lower than the target of USD $12.5 billion by the year as per the plan. Similarly, the company's operating margin target of 15% isn't expected to be achieved going by its latest outlook. These outcomes reduce confidence in the company.

At the same time, the fact that it was bang on about the financials for 2024 can't be ignored either. Neither can the fact that it upgraded its EPS guidance, and the actual numbers still surpassed it.

What next?

Owing to PVH's latest earnings numbers and expectation of improved revenue performance in 2025, there's still some reason to be positive on the stock. This is particularly important, as there's now a clear disconnect between the market multiples and the fundamentals. It might even make some gains this year on this account.

However, the equity markets and broader macro environment aren't favorably placed to support the consumer discretionary sector right now. These factors alone can negatively impact even stocks of high performing companies, so PVH stock is at even greater risk. I'm downgrading it to hold, on account of these developments. It would be good to wait and watch how the overall scenario evolves before buying it right now.

data.title: Lowe's May Face More Pain - Tariff Headwinds Not Priced In
data.description:
LOW May Face Further Macro Headwinds, With FY2025 Being A Painful Trough Year

We previously covered Lowe's Companies, Inc. (NYSE:LOW) in February 2024, discussing its robust performance metrics across growing profit margins, excellent shareholder returns, and lifting market sentiments as the Fed looked to pivot by H1'24.

Combined with the normalizing macroeconomy and recovery of resale housing market, we had believed that the stock offered a viable investment thesis then, significantly aided by the fair valuations and the management's intensified efforts to deliver improved one-stop-shop product offerings, expanded fulfilment options, and enhanced in-store/ digital experience.

LOW 1Y Stock Price

Since that Buy rating, LOW has charted an impressive upward rally by +20.1% to new 52 week heights of $287, before drastically pulling back by December 2024 as the Fed offers a hawkish rate cut, with the same pullback also observed in its peer and the wider market in varying degrees.

It is apparent by now that the inflation remains highly sticky, with the intensified tariff/ trade war and likely higher for longer interest rates already triggering recessionary risks, with the culmination of pessimistic developments contributing to the heightened CBOE Volatility Index and the market-wide selloff since mid-February 2025.

This is worsened by the still elevated 30Y Fixed Rate Mortgage Averages at 6.65% by March 27, 2025 and the 15Y rate at 5.89%, up from the September 19, 2024 bottom levels of 6.09%/ 5.15% and the 2019 averages of 4.13%/ 3.28%, respectively.

With borrowing costs still expensive and home prices still expensive, it is unsurprising why the market has turned pessimistic surrounding home improvement stocks, LOW included, attributed to the likely impacted DIY discretionary spending and Pro sales performance.

The same mixed demand has been observed in LOW's FQ4'24 earnings performance with comparable sales of +0.2% YoY, aided by the 100 basis points benefit from "storm-related demand from Hurricanes Helene and Milton" as "homeowners began the shift from securing their properties and clean-up towards recovery and rebuilding."

Otherwise, DIY discretionary spending remains impacted in FY2024, with 2025 likely to bring forth more "near term market uncertainty around the timing of an inflection in the home improvement market, especially for larger ticket discretionary spend."

The Consensus Forward Estimates

For now, LOW has already offered a mixed FY2025 guidance, with revenues of $84B at the midpoint (+0.3% YoY), comparable sales growth of +0.5% YoY, operating margins of 12.35% (-0.15 points YoY), and adj EPS of $12.275 (+0.3% YoY).

Given the inflated net debt levels at -$33.35B (+3.8% YoY/ -111% from FY2019 levels), the bloated inventory levels at $17.4B (+3% YoY/ +32.1% from FY2019 levels), and the impacted inventory turnover ratio at 3.2x (compared to a year ago at 3.4x and FY2019 levels of 3.73x), it is apparent that the company's turnaround process is likely to be prolonged.

Despite the impacted sales, LOW aims to "ramp up new store builds" with 2025 potentially bringing forth "five to 10 new stores," underscoring why its FY2025 Free Cash Flow generation is likely to disappoint, attributed to the higher capex by +30.2% YoY.

These reasons may be why the consensus forward estimates appear to be mixed in FY2025, with any meaningful top/ bottom-line recovery only likely from FY2026 onwards, as similarly observed in the prolonged normalization in the inflation rate through 2027.

Even so, we believe that LOW remains well capitalized to cover $2.6B in annualized dividend obligations, on top of the ongoing share retirement with -2% of its float retired over the LTM and -26.9% since FY2019, as the consensus continue to project robust cash flow generation ahead.

LOW Valuations

Despite the headwinds, LOW continues to trade at stable FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 18.78x, compared to the 5Y mean of 17.87x and 10Y mean of 17.75x, aided by the moderation from the 1Y mean of 20.11x.

At the same time, LOW is still trading attractively relative to its peer, Home Depot (HD) at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 24.35x.

The same conclusion is also observed in LOW's cheaper FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 4.32x against HD at 6.03x, despite the notable premium against their 5Y means of 3.88x/ 3.44x and 10Y means of 1.10x/ 1.69x, respectively, attributed to their intermediate term impacted adj EPS growth profile during the challenging year ahead.

So, Is LOW Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

LOW 5Y Stock Price

For now, LOW has lost much of its H1'24 gains, as the stock appears to bounce at the support levels of $220s while retesting the next resistance levels of $230s ranges.

Even then, given the ongoing market volatility, it is uncertain if the recent recovery may last, since the company's prospects may be impacted upon the intensification in reciprocal tariffs by the new US administration, worsened by the latest "proposal to impose a 20% tariff on most imports."

If anything, readers must note that LOW "source, both directly and indirectly, a portion of the products we sell from foreign manufacturers, with China and Mexico being the dominant import sources" for "our private branded and national brand merchandise."

These headwinds are likely to impact its profit margins assuming that the management chooses to absorb the additional costs, or sales, assuming that the additional costs are passed on to consumers at a time of tightened discretionary spending.

This is especially since the tariffs are "not explicitly included or incorporated in our guide in terms of what’s recently been enacted," with it potentially triggering a performance miss against the management's FY2025 guidance.

At the same time, readers may want to note that LOW is trading at a premium compared to our fair value estimates of $217.80, based on the management's FY2025 adj EPS guidance of $12.275 and the 10Y P/E mean of 17.75x.

Based on the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $15.01, there appears to be a minimal margin of safety to our long-term price target of $266.40 as well.

As a result of the near-term uncertainties and minimal upside potential, we prefer to prudently rate the LOW stock as a Hold (Neutral) here.

data.title: MercadoLibre: The Giant Redefining Consumption In Latin America
data.description:
Thesis Investment

MercadoLibre's (NASDAQ:MELI) growth has been led both by the expansion of the marketplace, as well as by the consolidation of its fintech ecosystem, which is a very relevant piece in its strategy. Mercado Pago has evolved from a payments platform to an ecosystem that promotes financial inclusion in a region with a low level of banking services. Its increase in assets under management and the massive adoption of its paid account reveal the trust of users and its potential to continue winning participation in the digital financial sector.

In addition, it has demonstrated an extraordinary ability to adapt to changes in the macroeconomic background. Regardless of operating in markets with high volatility, it has managed to maintain the growth in income and profitability, higher than the performance of the sector. Its cash generation permits it to continue investing in infrastructure, technology and logistics, reinforcement its leadership and creating entry barriers for competitors.

Although it faces regulatory and competition risks, the strength of its business model and the integration of its services provide it a competitive advantage. With a projected growth of 30% in the coming years and a profitability above the sector average, MercadoLibre is consolidating itself as one of the best options to invest in the digital expansion of Latin America. Because of this, I recommend MELI as a Strong Buy.

Overview

MercadoLibre is the largest e-commerce platform in Latin America, with participation in more than 18 countries, with Brazil, Mexico and Argentina being the largest markets.

In its early stages, in 1999, the firm wanted to connect buyers and sellers in the online market. Shortly after going on the market, regional expansion started and in 2007 it went public on NASDAQ.

Beginning in 2010, the development of the business model towards new services in the ecosystem was consolidated with Mercado Pago as a payment platform, Mercado Envío, to solve logistics, and Mercado Ads and Shop, developed to offer more solutions to sellers and advertisers.

These integrated services made MercadoLibre the leader in the sector, a leadership that was highlighted starting in 2020, determined mainly by the common use of Mercado Pago during the COVID-19 pandemic and the growing regional financial digitalization. Because of this, the company remains to invest in innovation, trying to increase its ecosystem and provide more and better solutions to users.

MELI has enormous growth potential, since, according to Statista estimates, the region has approximately 300 million digital buyers, a figure that is expected to increase more than 15% by 2027.

According to this study, growth between 2023 and 2028 would be led by Mexico (+86%), Brazil (+80%) and Argentina (+76%). In addition, there will be strong growth in all countries in the region for the period under study.

On the other hand, given the relative importance of fintech in the MELI ecosystem, it must be taken into account that by 2025 digital payments will increase by around 80%, as a result of the expansion of Internet access and the use of smartphones, which will promote financial inclusion.

Along these lines, it is estimated that the digital payments industry in LatAm will grow by around 7% in the next 5 years, above the 5% globally.

In this context, there is no doubt that the company is very well positioned to continue its expansion and diversification, not only in e-commerce, but also in its growing fintech segment.

Main results

According to the data presented by the company, during Q4 2024, net and financial income totaled $6.1 billion, +37% YoY (+96% in constant currency, without FX effects). For FY 2024, revenues reached $21 billion (+38% YoY and +86% in constant terms).

For 4Q 2024, operating income totaled $820 million, with a margin of 13.5% and net income reached $639 million (10.5% margin). For FY 2024, operating income was $2.6 billion (+6% YoY), and net income ascended $1.9 billion (+65% YoY).

MELI ended 2024 with a low debt level and a solid cash position. Net debt went from $1.5 billion in Q4 2023 to $2 billion in Q4 2024, thus, the Net Debt / Adjusted EBITDA ratio was 0.63x compared to 0.55x in the same quarter of the previous year.

In 4Q 2024, available cash and investments raised from $3.8 billion to $4.8 billion (+25.6% yoy).

The gross merchandise volume [GMV] reached $14.5 billion in 4Q 2024, growing 8% YoY (+56% in constant currency), and in FY 2024 it exceeded $50 billion. Considering the two countries with the highest sales volume, GMV grew in constant terms, by 32% in Brazil and 28% in Mexico.

Considering payments, it reached a Total Payment Volume [TPV] of $58.9 billion in 4Q 2024 (+33% YoY and +49% in constant terms).

Monthly active users (MAUs) rose 34% YoY, reaching 61.2 million. Additionally, assets under management [AUM] grew 129% YoY, getting $10.6 billion, due to the success of the yield account.

Valuation

MercadoLibre presents a P/E of 50.54x and an EV/EBITDA of 35.76x, high valuation ratios. However, when analyzed jointly with the company's other metrics, they show the market's confidence in the company's future growth, because year after year it has exceeded expectations. Likewise, given the leadership in the region in e-commerce and fintech, both exponential growth sectors, indicate that these values can be considered a long-term investment with strong appreciation potential.

In order to illustrate the company's valuation potential, revenue grew by 37.5% during the last year (15 times above the 2.4% of the sector), and it is highlighted that this performance has been sustained over time, with a CAGR of 55.3% in the last five years. In addition, a growth of 30% is estimated for the coming years.

As for returns, the earnings are impressive. Diluted EPS grew by 93.6% in 2024, and is expected to increase by an average of 30% annually in the coming years. While, regarding profitability, it has also had excellent results: the EBIT and EBITDA margins of 12.7% and 15.6%, respectively, are well above the sector average, indicating excellent management in operational efficiency. Additionally, the ROE of 51.5%, more than 350% higher than the sector average, highlights MELI as a generator of value for shareholders.

In net terms, MELI presents a profitability margin of 9.2%, which indicates that the company is not only growing very quickly, but also maximizing results in a highly competitive scenario. In addition to this, cash flow generation allows the company to continue investing in its expansion, without compromising its finances.

Risks

One of the main risks that the company faces is associated with having Latin America as its base of operations, which means frequently facing changes in taxes, regulations and economic policies. Argentina and Brazil, among others, might impose restrictions in the exchange market or new rules for trade and financial services.

On the other hand, it is well known that the region is very unstable in macroeconomic terms, which is why inflation, devaluations, and economic crises affect consumer purchasing power and, consequently, MELI's sales on the platform. The firm's exposure to unstable markets could generate greater volatility in its income.

Even though it is the main company in the region, it has giant global competitors such as Amazon (AMZN) and Alibaba (BABA), as well as coexisting with smaller companies that are trying to obtain a part in the Latin American market, in all the segments in which MELI operates, which forces it to constantly invest in promotions, technology and logistics to keep its position.

Likewise, it must be taken into account that Mercado Pago is key to the growth of MELI, but it faces complications. In some countries, cash payments remain to dominate, and regulation on financial services could tauten, affecting their profitability. In addition, the growth in loans arranged implies risks if delinquencies rise.

Conclusions

MELI offers a strong value proposition for investors. Its sustained growth, superior profitability and the ability to generate robust cash flow position it as one of the best opportunities in the Latin American market. Although the stock has a relatively high valuation, the potential for future growth, combined with its leadership in the sector, justifies my Strong Buy.

The company is well positioned to continue its expansion, not only in e-commerce, but also in its growing fintech segment, giving it a substantial competitive advantage. With an excellent ability to generate shareholder value, in my opinion, MELI is a long-term buy recommendation for those seeking to take advantage of the growth of digital commerce in Latin America.

data.title: HA Sustainable Infrastructure: Excellent Portfolio Yield And Cash Flow
data.description:
The Trump administration’s tariffs are spooking the markets, to say the least, with all three major U.S. indices down sharply since the start of the year. That’s why investors seeking to shelter their capital may want to consider domestic energy producers, as they are better insulated from direct pressures brought upon by tariffs. What’s even better is if said energy companies produce for domestic consumption, thereby offering protection from reciprocal tariffs from other countries.

This brings me to HA Sustainable Infrastructure (NYSE:HASI), which fits the example stated above. I last covered HASI back in January, highlighting its strong operating fundamentals, robust investment pipeline, and attractive valuation and yield. The stock has risen by 6% since my last piece, far outperforming the 6.3% decline in the S&P 500 (SPY) over the same timeframe.

As shown below, HASI has seen an overall upward trajectory since the start of this year, and yet still trades in the lower half of its 52-week range while carrying a 5.8% yield.

In this article, I revisit HASI including recent business performance and outlook and discuss why HASI remains appealing, especially in the current macroeconomic landscape, so let’s dive in!

Why HASI?

HA Sustainable Infrastructure is a leading investor in climate solutions. It finances and manages assets in clean energy solutions. Its business model is built around long-term recurring revenue streams. HASI’s assets include grid connected renewables (solar, wind), behind-the-meter energy efficiency solutions, sustainable infrastructure (renewable natural gas), and asset management.

It's worth noting that renewable energy isn’t meant to fully replace traditional energy sources, but rather serve as a complement to what management believes to be an ‘all-of-the-above’ strategy. This is supported by the following projection by the U.S. EIA which estimates growth in both natural gas and renewable energy through 2050. In particular, renewable energy is expected to grow to 44% of the energy mix by 2050, with much of that growth coming from solar and wind, as shown below.

Meanwhile, HASI continues to demonstrate robust results, with adjusted EPS growing by 10% in 2024 to $2.45. This was driven by strong recurring revenue from the $6.6 billion managed assets portfolio, as well as strategic capital deployment into diversified renewable assets. As shown below, HASI’s managed assets have grown by an impressive 90% since 2020.

Notably, HASI achieved an average portfolio yield of over 10.5% on $2.3 billion of new investments last year. Grid-connected solar, FTN (fuels, transport and nature), and public sector investments represented the top 3 investment categories for HASI last year. HASI’s yield on new portfolio assets grew from 9% to 10.5% last year and this contributed to 40 basis points of growth in the portfolio yield to 8.3%. As shown below, HASI has been able to effectively navigate both lower and higher interest rates environments with either a stable or growing portfolio yield over the past 6 years.

Management is guiding for adjusted EPS growth of 8-10% annually through 2027. This includes growth projects that include RNG (renewable natural gas), sustainable transportation and other carbon-neutral infrastructure. Management also plans to pursue international opportunities selectively through leveraging of existing client relationships. HASI also has opportunities to grow its SunStrong management platform, which takes on less risk through its servicing business, as noted during the last earnings call:

Our approach is working and our first priority is to grow the core business. Two, we will also showcase our adaptability. While uncertainty is not optimal, we do anticipate opportunity to come from it. A great example is the SunPower bankruptcy, while an unfortunate event, our SunStrong platform assumed the SunPower servicing business and we are excited about the growth prospects of this business. And finally, we have a strong track record of identifying new areas to invest. As such, we anticipate investing additional time and dollars explore a new growth paths and in particular, opening up new end-markets.

Over the long run, HASI is poised to benefit from continued demand growth for electricity. Management pointed out that demand for renewable power isn’t driven by the Inflation Reduction Act, but rather by its low-cost and underlying demand. According to McKinsey, US electricity demand will double over the next 25 years to more than 8,000 terawatt hours by 2050. Much of this growth is driven by data center load, EV penetration and electrification of buildings as shown below.

Importantly, HASI is supported by $1.8 billion in total liquidity. It carries a debt-to-equity ratio of 1.8x, sitting within management’s target range of 1.5 to 2.0x with 100% fixed or hedged rates. HASI also has BBB-/Baa3 investment grade credit ratings from Fitch and Moody’s, and a BB+ rating with positive outlook from S&P, which puts it on track for an upgrade.

HASI currently yields an appealing 5.8% and the dividend is well-covered by a 68% payout ratio. The dividend has a 4.4% 5-year CAGR and management expects to gradually lower the payout ratio to 50% by 2030 so that it can tap retained capital to fund growth.

Lastly, I continue to see value in HASI at the current price of $29.21 with a forward PE of 11.0. This sits far below its normal PE of 19.2, as shown below.

Analysts estimate 8% to 10% annual EPS growth over the next 3 years, which, I believe, is reasonable considering the aforementioned robust demand for electricity and HASI’s rising portfolio yield. This, combined with the 5.8% dividend yield and potential for a reversion to the mean valuation, could result in potential double-digit total returns annually.

Risks to HASI include perceived notions that it’s reliant on government subsidies. However, the lowering of renewable energy costs over the past decade has made underlying economics a core driver for renewable energy growth. Moreover, HASI’s development pipeline is insulated from policy changes due to safe harbor provisions. While the production and delivery of HASI’s energy isn’t subject to cross-border trade, it could be impacted by higher input material costs. This could hit the bottom line if they can’t be passed onto its customers.

Investor Takeaway

HA Sustainable Infrastructure stands out as a resilient and well-positioned investment in the current macro environment, offering exposure to the growing demand for clean energy while avoiding direct exposure to tariffs and international trade risks. It has a strong portfolio yielding over 10.5% on new investments, solid EPS growth, and a well-covered 5.8% dividend. Plus, with a valuation far below its historical norm, HASI offers an appealing combination of income and growth potential.

data.title: Deutsche Bank: A Great Time To Cash Out, As Upside Seems Limited This Time (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Intro: A Very Global, And Very German Bank

Germany has been in the headlines a lot lately when it comes to financial markets, not only because a former BlackRock (BLK) lawyer is set to be the new Chancellor, but also some news pointing to a shrinking economy and recession.

So, I thought it would be a great idea that today's article cover a major bank based in Germany, Deutsche Bank (NYSE:DB), which also has its own tower in Lower Manhattan as well and many Wall Streeters reading this should be familiar with that location, actually.

Back in June 2023, I saw the potential of this stock when it was just under $11 and called it a strong buy, and since then, it has gone up +129%. That's quite the price appreciation, I would say, for those who bought at the time, and probably my best-performing buy rating so far on this platform. I then continued to be bullish in my Oct. 2023 follow-up article, and since that one it is up too.

What drove my positive sentiment was what I thought was undervaluation at the time, conservative risk management, and a strong capital position.

In today's article, I will look at several additional data points and angles, putting greater weight on what could drive future upside and downside potential for this bank whose Seeking Alpha profile describes as being in the business of "corporate and investment banking, private clients, and asset management products and services."

Thesis: Going Bearish This Time Around

In today's article, I will argue why it may be time to sell this stock and take profits off the table, awaiting its next major dip below average before buying again.

While it does not show overvaluation within its sector, the firm has seen positive net client money inflows, and Fitch gave it stellar credit ratings of A-, but that's where the party on the top floor of Wall Street ends for me.

It also has unremarkable loan growth lately, several business segments that could see downside risk if the negative macro forecasts for 2025 transpire, including impacts to M&A, equity markets, loan demand and money flows.

In addition, it has no proven 10-year dividend growth and is low yield, not super attractive to many dividend investors in my opinion, and not only is it trading practically at 5-year highs, but it has significant exposure to assets that pose increasing trends of loan impairment risk, not to mention an unstellar profit margin among peers.

Current Macro Picture For 2025 Less Confident Than Before, And It Could Impact This Bank

In this section, let's kick things off by considering what macro-level factors could impact this firm positively or negatively, and for that, we should first get a better idea of the firm's "Global Hausbank" business strategy.

Like I mentioned in one of my earlier articles on this firm, it is well diversified across a portfolio of business segments, and the diversification can lower the risk of over-concentration in one product or segment, unless of course, all segments are impacted negatively by macro events.

A key role of its Corporate Bank segment is financing business loans, so I imagine demand for loans comes into play, and poor loan growth is a negative. A key role of the Investment Bank segment would be origination and advisory (O&A), so one factor that could affect that space is the growth or decline of mergers/acquisitions. One of the Private Bank's key roles is essentially managing wealth clients, while the Asset Management segment could depend on client money inflows as well as equities markets going up.

A potential negative factor for the M&A space in the first half of 2025 is what Reuters described in early March as a "current chill on dealmaking, attributing it to a lack of predictability coming from Washington," with further data indicating "M&A activity in the U.S. during the first two months of this year was the slowest in more than two decades."

As for asset management, it can be impacted by client fund flows and also market values. On a macro-level, late-February data from Morningstar suggested that "dollars largely flowed to taxable-bond and nontraditional-equity funds," and more importantly, "five of the 10 category groups suffered outflows, including two of the three traditional equity groups, suggesting investors remain somewhat cautious."

In terms of equity markets, we could use the S&P500 index as a benchmark, and lately, it seems forecasts for 2025 have been somewhat downgraded, which I think could negatively impact this firm.

For example, a late-March article in Yahoo! Finance cited a Goldman Sachs analyst saying the following after the bank downgraded its forecast for the S&P 500 this year:

These estimates incorporate downward revisions to both earnings growth and valuations, reflecting a weaker base case economic growth backdrop, higher uncertainty, and higher recession risk.

So, investors should consider this near-term macroeconomic backdrop and the potential downside risk it could present to specific business segments of this bank.

Recent Client Inflows A Positive, But Interest-Earning Assets Growth Unremarkable

From its most recent earnings release on Jan. 30th, two notable business segments to mention are the asset management and private bank, which saw "net inflows of €55 billion, during 2024; this is expected to drive revenue growth in these businesses in future periods."

I believe growth in inflows in such fee-driven segments could drive future upside, since fees are charged on those monies managed by this bank.

However, one look at its income statement will tell you that interest income is a much bigger moneymaker for the firm than fees-driven income, and in fact, net interest income made up 70% of total revenue in the quarter ending Dec. 2024.

With that said, I am looking for evidence of loan growth since that could drive future interest revenue growth, but also overall recent growth in interest-earning assets since interest income can also come from fixed-income assets held like bonds.

From the table below in the Q4 presentation, we can see what I would call an unremarkable growth of 1% YoY in loans (both vs FY 2024 and vs Q4 2023) but also a mere 3% YoY growth in avg. interest earning assets vs FY23:

So, future upside when it comes to future revenue growth may not be that impressive, and in fact when compared to three global banking peers: UBS (UBS), Morgan Stanley (MS), and Goldman Sachs (GS), it seems Deutsche trails behind in expected fwd rev growth;

Profit Margin Could Use Improvement, But Cost Efficiencies Showing Bright Spots

Moving on to the bottom line, I would mention that in the last quarterly results the firm actually saw a YoY decline in net income, so I'm sure investors and analysts noticed and when I talk about the share price later I expect they reacted negatively.

The margin is usually a key metric I like to point to as an indicator of efficiency in turning a profit. When it comes to this bank, although its net income margin is 14.6%, it trails the sector average which is nearly 23%.

Further, compared to the peers mentioned earlier its profit margin trails behind that of Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs who are considerably higher.

At the same time, though, it is only fair to mention that the firm has been making an effort to be more cost-efficient, and they mentioned progress of this in their Q4 results release:

Measures include optimization of the bank’s platform in Germany and workforce reductions, notably in non-client-facing roles. As at year-end 2024, savings either realized or expected from completed efficiency measures grew to € 1.8 billion, or approximately 75% of the program’s expected total savings.

Also worth mentioning on a positive note in this regard, is that the firm's cost outlook for FY25 expects some rising expenses due to business growth. However, we can also "expect significant reduction in nonoperating costs in FY 2025 from non-repeat of FY 2024 specific litigation items and normalization of remaining nonoperating costs," according to its Q4 presentation.

A Low-Yield Dividend Stock With Unstable Growth, But Recent Improvement

This stock is being included in my dividend ideas for the month, and so in this section I want to touch on what this stock presents for dividend investors.

First, I would say for readers less familiar with this stock, or with foreign-based companies, Deutsche Bank actually has its shares listed directly on the NYSE as well as in Frankfurt, and not in the form of an ADR like some foreign companies are.

However, its dividend is an annual one so not something to depend on for quarterly income, but its next ex-date is coming up soon in late May.

At a yield of 2%, it's not what I call a high-yield stock (which would be above 5% or better), but also does not have a proven 10-year dividend growth so if that is a factor you are looking for this is probably not the best dividend stock, although it seems to have been on an improvement trend since 2022:

Thus, if you are simply following the 5-year dividend growth trend and not the 10-year, and it continues, it could mean growth in annual dividend income. However, for a bank continuing on a path of cost-cutting and trying to improve its margins, I am not yet convinced the dividend hike trend will continue necessarily, so for this reason, I am lukewarm when it comes to its dividend case.

Modest Debt/Equity vs Peers Paired With Strong Credit Rating, But Impaired Loans Pose Risk

When it comes to balance sheet risk for this type of business, what comes to mind is that it has a significant chunk of its assets tied to its loan portfolio. In fact, nearly $508B in gross loans, which exposes the firm to potential loan loss risk, as well as nearly $233B of investment securities, so the fixed-income assets could present interest-rate risk.

On the liability side, the firm has about $118B of long-term debt, but I think that debt should be viewed in the context of equity, so here is how its Debt/Equity ratio looks vs peers:

From the above data, Deutsche Bank's D/E is actually the lowest among this peer group at 1.88, and while it may be higher than other companies I have rated here it is important to consider it in the context of its sector and similar peers, so in this case it is actually not high and seems to have declined in the last 3 years as the chart line shows.

Considering an additional angle, let's see what rating agency Fitch had to say in the most recent rating commentary I found from Feb. 4th, which highlighted the investment-grade "A-" rating on the firm's debt issuance. However, it reiterated what I mentioned above and that is exposure to at least some loan loss risk, saying the following:

The impaired loans ratio rose to 3.3% (end-2023: 2.8%). These higher impairments mainly resulted from the EUR36 billion non-recourse commercial real estate CRE portfolio, collection backlogs in German retail banking, and a small number of large defaults in the wealth management and corporate loan books.

A Share Price Near Its 5-Year High, But No Signs of Overvaluation

Although I expected shares to dive after the Q4 results, and there was indeed a small drop of 4% after results came out, according to Euronews, after creating the price chart below I was indeed surprised that this stock has soared past its 200-day simple moving average and is near its 5-year high:

So, it could be that investors have shrugged off the YoY profit declines posted, and are bullish about the future of this firm. Consider its momentum chart also, which shows a 1-year price return of 53%, vs the mere 9.2% return of the S&P 500.

Interestingly, this is despite all the headlines of late about tariffs causing economic headwinds both in the US and Europe, and some of the macro forecasts I mentioned earlier.

In terms of valuation metrics, the data seems limited on Seeking Alpha, the preferred data source I use, but we can see that the trailing P/E of 12.53 is relatively in line with the sector average, while the trailing price/book value of 0.56 is actually lower than the sector average.

What could explain bullishness on this stock is sector data showing that financial services have been among the top performers on both a 1 year and 3-year basis, and so far, in 2025, the sector has remained positive despite the recent volatility and dramatic headlines about tariffs.

Conclusion: Time To Pull The Plug And Move On, Return At Next Dip

Ok, so after my prior 2 bullish calls, I am ready to pull back and call it a sell today, as it appears to be time to take the nice profit off the table, particularly for folks who saw their shares go up +129% since my bullish call.

Consider all the upside and downside factors discussed today, and the weighted scores I gave them, in my rating sheet below:

From that rating sheet, the following visualization points to the downside outweighing the upside factors in this case, justifying a sell.

Some factors that could challenge my bearish call on Deutsche Bank could be that the macro forecasts turn out to have been overly negative, and also the next few quarters the firm sees significant new loan growth and more client inflows, as well as strong M&A deals. However, there are too many of those factors that would all have to be favorable, and let's not forget the significant competition in the investment banking space from peers like Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs, among several others.

I welcome your commentary on this stock today, which is my first downgrade of the month, and my question is, where you think banks will be heading this year?

As always, be sure to follow this article as part of my ongoing theme on this website called Financial Markets with Albert Anthony, where I pick dividend stocks to include in a diversified portfolio.

data.description:
Performance Commentary

Fund Inception 8/18/05. Portfolio performance reflects Broadleaf’s Growth Equity Composite, described more fully under the caption “Performance Disclosures.” You are urged to read that information in its entirety in connection with any evaluation of Broadleaf’s performance statistics. All figures are shown net of actual fees. Any assumed fees have been calculated on a pro forma basis, reflecting the highest fee levels that Broadleaf would charge clients per our disclosures in Part II of our Form ADV.

Growth stocks had a tough relative quarter, as the market punished many of the big winners of the past few years. This “momentum” unwind resulted in a tough quarter for the BGEP.

Market Review & Outlook

We have written more than usual this quarter, given the volatility of the market and the flip- flopping developments in the economy. The rapid change in daily news flow has us on our toes, however, we feel that it makes sense to be more gradual with portfolio changes to the BGEP, as we discern what is true and what is narrative. The market is best described as moody, and we get a new mood each day. We believe it is important to listen to what the market is saying, but when it is hard to get a clear signal, it doesn’t always pay to obsess over every movement.

Our goal for this update will be to report facts on the economy, as we have recently given our opinions on the current tech and political environment. We will try our best to avoid AI or politics but feel obliged to mention that we are more constructive than the polarizing news flow. While at risk of being boring, sometimes a more sober approach is warranted.

Note: In the last couple of days we have spent preparing these thoughts, multiple highly respected market strategists have increased their odds of a recession, showing just how fragile the current environment is. We would note that most of these recession-call probabilities still reside below 50%, but the pace at which they are ticking up is notable. In addition, 4/2/2025 has been marked, “Liberation Day” by President Trump, which might represent yet another drastic shift in market conditions.

Earnings

Q4 2024 Earnings were rather strong, as earnings were up 13% in aggregate for large caps. Earnings also beat expectations rather handily, outpacing analyst expectations by about 7%, despite weakness in more cyclically oriented sectors such as energy and industrials. However, as we know, the market doesn’t care about the past – but cares about what is next.

The first look at annual guidance for 2025 was solid, but perhaps a bit conservative, as company management teams warned about the uncertainty in the business outlook, primarily due to tariffs. It is very common for management teams to guide conservatively in the first quarter of each year, and then set their sights on beating these conservative expectations.

There’s no doubt that given the volatile environment, management teams may find extra prudence to be wise, and this has been reflected as full-year earnings estimates have come down a hair since the beginning of the year.

If we drill down into the quarterly earnings expectations for 2025, the 1 st quarter has taken the largest hit as investors expect that the volatility of the current environment will bleed through in the upcoming quarter. However, a strong back half of the year is still expected, suggesting that analysts and market strategists are still expecting the current situation to improve as the year progresses.

Summary: Strong, resilient expectations in 2025.

Employment

The key cog for the economy is typically employment. Federal layoffs have thus far been absorbed relatively well, as weekly jobless claims have remained relatively low. If companies begin to suspect that economic uncertainty will not be resolved quickly, they will likely begin more significant layoffs to defend earnings.

Most management teams know that it is hard to make long-term strategic decisions in a whiplash kind of environment. Given this, companies are not hiring at the rapid pace they once were, nor are they shedding many employees. Many companies are simply maintaining the status quo, and as such, employees are more content to stay at their current jobs. As we saw a few years ago in 2022, we are not likely to have a recession unless further cracks emerge in employment.

Summary: Stable

The Consumer

Consumer sentiment surveys hit the lowest levels since 2022, as consumers expect inflation to pick up again for a multitude of reasons. Many management teams affirmed that reinflation fears may be justified, with the prices paid for goods beginning to trickle up. These management teams also indicated that the consumer is selective, but they are still active. Who doesn’t love a good deal? The most recent economic data from last Friday, March 28th seemed to affirm these observations, with inflation trickling up month over month, and with actual consumer spending less depressed than how consumers are feeling.

Note: Consumer sentiment remains well below pre-pandemic levels. Consumers HATE inflation.

Note: Actual spending is as steady as it gets, save for the 2020 craziness.

One of our favorite economists, Ed Yardeni, likes to point out that consumers love to spend, especially when they are depressed, so long as they remain employed. While surveys have value, it is important to watch what consumers do, not just listen to what they say.

Consumers have remained relatively depressed ever since the COVID pandemic, with actual consumer spending looking rather resilient. The latest data suggests a slight bump in the road, but no major trend change at this point. It is important to listen to how consumers feel, as it can indicate future weakness, but this has not been a very reliable signal over the past few years.

Summary: Watch What I Do, Not What I Say.

GDP

The Atlanta Fed puts out a forecast on real GDP growth expectations for the current quarter. This forecast has a pretty strong reputation as being a reliable predictor of GDP growth for the current quarter. The most recent quarterly Real GDP forecast is expected to shrink by 1.8%, which is certainly not a good sign. However, a deeper dive into the components of this figure shows that net exports are solely responsible for the decline. Put in plain English, the economy is still growing, but companies are importing cars, electronics, and a whole host of goods before tariff day, which is leading to negative Q1 growth estimates. Companies will not likely import at such a rapid rate once tariffs are in place, making a Q2 bounce possible, but hard to predict.

If you are in the market for a new car, it might not make sense to wait much longer.

Summary: Declining. Tariff pull-forward clouds data.

Uncertainty:

Economic policy uncertainty is near levels seen during COVID and during the Great Recession. This is surprising, as economic panic felt more palpable out on Main Street during those two prior peaks. While the current environment has raised a few eyebrows, we think that the uncertainty brought upon by tariffs and DOGE can be resolved rather quickly, for better or worse. We have no illusions that tariffs are good for consumers, but we do hope for more straightforward messaging and policy in due time.

It definitely seems like the business environment is on high alert right now, as M&A has slowed considerably. One of our favorite investing parables is, “Risk is where uncertainty is growing.” We won’t discount this one, but also think that self-inflicted dramas are more easily placated. In the meantime, risk is certainly elevated.

Summary: There’s a lot of fear out there.

Recently I have noticed in personal conversations and on social media that a debate has sparked on the wisdom of buying this dip. Many are happily buying it, throwing their hard-earned cash in potential harm’s way while other investors take risk off the table, fearing the worst is yet to come. This debate gets testy at times, as some investors question whether we can get yet another “V-shaped bounce.

Our crystal ball is as good as anyone else’s - and they are all pretty poor -, but we believe that both of these investors could be right. Being in the business of predicting recessions is tough and usually quite unprofitable work. We would remind folks that not long ago, it seemed like all of the smartest economists were calling for a recession that never came. Bear markets and volatility are a certainty of life, and they are the price we pay for the good times that will also no doubt, one day come.

A chart in my office shows the history of the US Economy. It serves as a reminder that we made it through a Civil War, the worst recession in US history (so bad it was called a Depression), two World Wars, a Cold War, the brink of nuclear catastrophe, endless panics, inflationary and deflationary regimes, and countless other periods of high uncertainty, just like we have today. I can also tell you that investors with a long-time horizon are happy with the forward returns from each of these uncertainty “peaks”. There is not much of a reason to think that this time will be different.

The real answer to the buy the dip question may be more of an emotional one. We feel that this could just be One Of Those Times, not inherently from a good or bad economy, but policy-induced anxiety. The solution to anxiety during these kinds of periods is usually altering one’s asset allocation – or buckling up for the ride.

We are coming off a long-term winning streak, and investors who began this run with us may find themselves today in a completely different financial situation than when it started. All the facts and charts in the world probably won’t change how you feel about your money, but we are still here to help. Our financial advisory team is one of the best in the business at managing the less quantitative, but more emotional intersection of behavioral investing and portfolio construction. Please do not hesitate to reach out to them if you have questions, or to me if you want to talk about some more epic charts and data!

About the Author

Pete is an Associate Portfolio Manager at Broadleaf, with a strong background in the financial services industry. He joined the firm in 2021, and has a proven track record of analyzing portfolio companies and identifying growth opportunities. Pete is a CPA and received his Chartered Financial Analyst( CFA®) designation in 2024. Pete lives in Hudson, Ohio with his wife Nicole.

Portfolio Characteristics

Portfolio Statistics

Investment Style

The Broadleaf Growth Equity Portfolio employs a concentrated growth style of investing, holding approximately 25-35 equity positions from a cross section of economic sectors. Morningstar would classify us as a large cap growth manager, but we will invest in select small and midsize companies as unique opportunities avail themselves. Sector exposures are strongly influenced by our views on three determinants of investment value, which we define as the economic cycle, the innovation cycle, and the credit cycle. Individual securities are ultimately selected on the basis of their long-term growth potential, profitability, and intrinsic value as measured by their free cash flow generating characteristics. Innovative new ideas and themes are of particular interest.

Investment Objective

The portfolio’s goal is to provide equity like returns and to outperform the S&P 500 over a three to five-year time horizon or full market cycle, utilizing a growth oriented investment style. The portfolio is suitable for investors seeking an exposure to a concentrated investment style which may be more volatile than the market as a whole. Investors should consider it as a portion of their investment portfolio within the context of their overall asset allocation and related investment goals.

Performance Disclosures

Results reflect the actual performance of Broadleaf’s Growth Equity Composite. Performance data is shown net of advisory fees and trading costs. Broadleaf may charge different advisory fees to clients based on several factors, but primarily based on the size of a client’s account. Broadleaf’s basic fee schedule is available on its Form ADV, Part II. Results reflect the reinvestment of dividends and distributions, if any. Leverage has not been utilized. The U.S. Dollar is the currency used to express performance.

Broadleaf’s growth Equity Composite includes all fully discretionary accounts utilizing our growth equity style of investing with a minimum initial account size of $250,000. (From firm inception to 6/30/2009 our minimum account size for composite inclusion was $250,000 and from 6/30/2009 to 6/30/2013, the minimum had been

$100,000. Historical results have not been updated retroactively to reflect changes in account minimums, but are reflected on a going forward basis.) To be included in the composite, an account must have been under management for at least one full quarter. If a significant cash flow in an underlying composite account during the quarter causes it to deviate from our intended growth style, we will remove the account for the period in which the significant cash event occurred. A significant cash flow is currently defined as 10% or more.

Total firm assets at quarter end were $462 million. Prior to January 5 th, 2006 the firm did not have any investment advisory clients. As a result, composite data prior to March 31 st, 2006 only reflects the performance of Doug MacKay’s personal retirement account.

The S&P 500 Index has been used for comparative benchmark purposes because the goal of the stated strategy is to provide equity-like returns and to outperform the S&P 500 over a three to five-year time horizon or full market cycle. The S&P 500 is a broad based index reflecting the performance of the equity market in general. The S&P 500 Index is based on total returns which includes dividends. We monitor the performance of our growth style of investing by comparing our results to those of other large cap growth peers. While we believe these are appropriate benchmarks to use for comparison purposes, it should be expected that the volatility of the Broadleaf Growth Equity Portfolio may be higher due to its concentrated nature.

Performance information since inception reflects actual performance of the composite over a period of greater than fifteen years. You are cautioned that information concerning comparative performance over this period of time may bear no relationship whatsoever to performance over other time periods. This information should not be regarded as in anyway representing the likely future performance of the portfolio in absolute terms or in comparison to the indices. Investment in securities, including mutual funds, involves risk of loss. Past performance is no guarantee of future returns.

Yield should not be used as an indication of the income that has or will be received. We do not consider yield as performance but instead as an attribute relating to the income production profile of the portfolio and its underlying assets.

Broadleaf Partners, LLC is a registered investment advisor with the Securities and Exchange Commission. The firm maintains a complete list and description of composites, which is available upon request.

Performance information contained in this document including any reference to the purchase or sale of a security, or a strategy, is not intended to constitute personalized investment advice. Personalized investment advice is always dependent on individual factors, involves risk and is not a guarantee that any investment will produce favorable results.
